# ChatMessageView
ChatMessageView helps you to create chat message view quickly like a typical chatting application.
Its a container view, so you can add any type of message such as TextView or any customize TextView, ImageView, etc.


## Features
1. Can have any child inside of it.
2. You can change color of `ChatMessageView` normal and pressed.
3. Adjustable arrow position

## Quick Start

1. Download [doozerd](https://github.com/ha/doozerd/downloads)
2. Unpack the archive and put `doozerd` in your `PATH`
3. Repeat for [doozer](https://github.com/ha/doozer/downloads)
4. Start a doozerd with a WebView listening on `:8080`

        $ doozerd -w ":8080"

5. Set a key and read it back

        $ echo "hello, world" | doozer add /message
        $ doozer get /message
        hello, world

6. Open <http://localhost:8080> and see your message

![doozer web view](doc/webview.png)

## How Does It Work?

Doozer is a network service. A handful of machines
(usually three, five, or seven) each run one doozer
server process. These processes communicate with each
other using a standard fully-consistent distributed
consensus algorithm. Clients dial in to one or more of
the doozer servers, issue commands, such as GET, SET,
and WATCH, and receive responses.

(insert network diagram here)

Each doozerd process has a complete copy of the
datastore and serves both read and write requests; there
is no distinguished "master" or "leader". Doozer is
designed to store data that fits entirely in memory; it
never writes data to permanent files. A separate tool
provides durable storage for backup and recovery.

## When Should I Use It?

Here are some example scenarios:

1. *Name Service*

    You have a set of machines that serve incoming HTTP
    requests. Due to hardware failure, occasionally one
    of these machines will fail and you replace it with a
    new machine at a new network address. A change to DNS
    data would take time to reach all clients, because
    the TTL of the old DNS record would cause it to
    remain in client caches for some time.

    Instead of DNS, you could use Doozer. Clients can
    subscribe to the names they are interested in, and
    they will get notified when any of those names’
    addresses change.

2. *Database Master Election*

    You are deploying a MySQL system. You want it to have
    high availability, so you add slaves on separate
    physical machines. When the master fails, you might
    promote one slave to become the new master. At any
    given time, clients need to know which machine is the
    master, and the slaves must coordinate with each
    other during failover.

    You can use doozer to store the address of the
    current master and all information necessary to
    coordinate failover.

3. *Configuration*

    You have processes on several different machines, and
    you want them all to use the same config file, which
    you must occasionally update. It is important that
    they all use the same configuration.

    Store the config file in doozer, and have the
    processes read their configuration directly from
    doozer.

## What can I do with it?

We have a detailed description of the [data model](doc/data-model.md).

For ways to manipulate or read the data, see the [protocol spec](doc/proto.md).

Try out doozer's fault-tolerance with some [fire drills](doc/firedrill.md).

## Similar Projects

Doozer is similar to the following pieces of software:

 * Apache Zookeeper <http://zookeeper.apache.org/>
 * Google Chubby <http://research.google.com/archive/chubby.html>

## Hacking on Doozer

 * [hacking on doozer](doc/hacking.md)
 * [mailing list][mail]

Doozer was created by Blake Mizerany and Keith Rarick.
Type `git shortlog -s` for a full list of contributors.

# Ninja-ide Is Not Just Another IDE.
**Ninja-IDE** is a cross-platform integrated development environment (IDE) that allows developers to create applications for any purpose making the task of writing software easier and more enjoyable. It's also a secret ninja agency but this doesn't matter right now.

## Platforms

On any system you want **Ninja-IDE**, you'll need to have this dependencies installed:

```bash
git clone git://github.com/ninja-ide/ninja-ide.git

python ninja-ide.py

"}, {"sample_repo_name": "cwilso/midi-synth", "content": "# MIDI Synth

This application is a analog synthesizer simulation built on the [Web Audio API](https://dvcs.w3.org/hg/audio/raw-file/tip/webaudio/specification.html).  It is very loosely based on the architecture of a [Moog Prodigy](http://www.vintagesynth.com/moog/prodigy.php) synthesizer, although this is a polyphonic synthesizer, and it lacks the oscillator sync and glide effects of the Prodigy.  (AKA: this is not intended to be a replication of the Prodigy, so pleased don't tell me how crappy a reproduction it is! :)

This uses my [Web MIDI Polyfill](https://github.com/cwilso/WebMIDIAPIShim) to add MIDI support via the [Web MIDI API](http://webaudio.github.io/web-midi-api/) - in fact, I partly wrote this as a test case for the polyfill and the MIDI API itself, so if you have a MIDI keyboard attached, check it out.  The polyfill uses Java to access the MIDI device, so if you're wondering why Java is loading, that's why.  It may take a few seconds for MIDI to become active - the library takes a while to load - but when the ring turns gray (instead of blue), it's ready.  If you have a native implementation of the Web MIDI API in your browser, the polyfill shouldn't load - at the time of this writing, Chrome Stable (from version 43) has the only such implementation. Earlier versions of Chrome (from version 33) can enable Web MIDI via chrome://flags/#enable-web-midi

You can try it out live at https://webaudiodemos.appspot.com/midi-synth/index.html.

Check it out, feel free to fork, submit pull requests, etc.

-Chris

Each doozerd process has a complete copy of the

## When Should I Use It?

2. *Database Master Election*

message.reasoning:
The user asks: "Among repositories that do not use Python, what proportion of their README.md files include copyright information?"

We need to:
1. Find repositories that do not use Python (from metadata_database.languages)
2. Check if their README.md files include copyright information (from artifacts_database.contents)
3. Calculate the proportion

First, let's understand the schema:

metadata_database (SQLite):
- languages: repo_name (str), language_description (str)
- licenses: repo_name (str), license (str)
- repos: repo_name (str), watch_count (int)

artifacts_database (DuckDB):
- contents: id (str), content (str), sample_repo_name (str), sample_ref (str), sample_path (str), sample_symlink_target (str), repo_data_description (str)
- commits: commit (str), tree (str), parent (str), author (str), committer (str), subject (str), message (str), trailer (str), difference (str), difference_truncated (bool), repo_name (str), encoding (str)
- files: repo_name (str), ref (str), path (str), mode (int), id (str), symlink_target (str)

The query plan:

1. Get list of repositories that do NOT use Python from metadata_database.languages
2. Get list of README.md files from artifacts_database.contents 
3. Filter README.md files to only those from repositories that don't use Python
4. For each README.md, check if it contains copyright-related information
5. Calculate proportion

Let me think about the complexity here. The datasets are large. We saw that the result for repos not using Python has 2,774,729 repositories. And we need to check their README.md files.

We should be strategic:

Step 1: Get the list of non-Python repositories
Step 2: Get all README.md files with their repository names
Step 3: Filter README.md files to only those from non-Python repositories
Step 4: For each README.md, check for copyright information
Step 5: Calculate the proportion

Copyright information can appear in various forms:
- The word "copyright" (case-insensitive)
- The symbol ©
- "All rights reserved"
- "Apache License"
- "MIT License"
- "GPL"
- etc.

Let's write the queries:


I'll proceed with implementing the specific steps to identify copyright-related content, focusing on systematic filtering and matching strategies across the large dataset.

Step 1 involves querying non-Python repositories. I noticed the previous query returned an extensive list of about 2.77 million repositories. To manage this, I'll optimize the approach by first retrieving README.md files and performing an intersection with non-Python repository lists. This strategic filtering will help reduce computational complexity.

The optimization plan is to carefully query the DuckDB contents table, ensuring we efficiently extract only the necessary README.md files for repositories not using Python. By narrowing the dataset before performing detailed analysis, we can significantly improve query performance and processing time.

The query structure suggests a targeted extraction method, focusing on specific repository names and files. The metadata indicates the results are substantial, with a preview showing multiple repository examples. I'll use these insights to develop an efficient data processing strategy that minimizes unnecessary computational overhead.

The approach involves first extracting a smaller subset of data to validate patterns and develop an accurate detection mechanism. This preliminary investigation will help establish a robust method for identifying copyright information across the full dataset without immediate full-scale processing.

The key steps involve carefully sampling repository contents, analyzing potential copyright indicators within the sample, and then designing a scalable technique that can be applied more broadly. By starting with a controlled examination, I can refine the detection logic before attempting comprehensive analysis.

The plan centers on developing a precise, adaptive strategy that evolves from initial small-scale testing to more extensive data processing, ensuring accuracy and efficiency throughout the investigation.

You are a data analysis agent. Use only the tools listed below to answer the user's query, based on the provided DATABASE DESCRIPTION for logical database names and their types (SQL or MongoDB), and the results of previous tool calls.

TOOLS (system will execute):
- query_db: run a SQL or Mongo query. Returns a list of JSON-serializable records or an error string.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>", "query": "<SQL or Mongo query>"}
- list_db: list tables or collections for a given database.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>"}
- execute_python: run Python code.
  Required args: {"code": "<python_code>"}
- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).
  Required args: {"answer": "<final plain-text answer>"}

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. After each tool call, its result will be stored in a storage under a key named after the tool call id (you will be told the key name). The next message will include both the result (or a preview if it's large) and the storage key name.
2. Inside execute_python code you may read storage entries using the provided key names, e.g., if the tool call id is 'functions.tool:id', you can access its result via `locals()['var_functions.tool:id']` in your code.
3. You cannot modify or reassign those storage-provided variables; you may read them and create new variables as needed.
4. If a tool result is large, the next message will include a preview (first 10000 characters) and the storage entry will be the .json file path (a string) where the full result is stored. To access the full result, your execute_python code must open and read that .json file.

KEY RULES (must follow exactly):
1. Always use tool calls. Do not output plain text, explanations, or reasoning.
2. Include all required arguments for the tool you call.
3. For query_db, always specify db_name and query. Refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION for db_name and query format.
4. For PostgreSQL, wrap mixed-case or uppercase column names in double quotes.
5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.
6. Use execute_python for data processing as needed. 
7. When using execute_python, your code will be quoted by triple double-quotes and passed as a string to `exec(...)` for execution in a Python 3.12 environment with only pandas and pyarrow installed. So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.
8. When using execute_python, your code must print the result at the end exactly as shown in the PRINT FORMAT section below. The printed result must be a string that can be successfully parsed by json.loads() without errors.

PRINT FORMAT (must match exactly):
----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----
print("__RESULT__:")
print(your_json_serializable_string_here)
----END PRINT FORMAT----

For simple types (int, float, str, bool, None), you may use json.dumps() to produce a valid JSON string.
For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.
9. Return the final answer only via a single return_answer tool call. Do not include extra text, explanation, or formatting.

EXAMPLES:
- query_db:
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "SELECT * FROM some_table LIMIT some_limit;"}} # for SQL databases
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "{"collection": "some_collection", "filter": {some_filter}, "projection": {some_projection}, "limit": some_limit}"}} # for MongoDB databases

- list_db:
{"tool": "list_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name"}}

- execute_python:
{"tool": "execute_python", "args": {"code": "import pandas as pd
# rl1 and rl2 are the keys of two JSON-serializable record lists in storage
df1 = pd.DataFrame(rl1)
df2 = pd.DataFrame(rl2)
result = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id').head(10).to_json(orient='records')
print('__RESULT__:')

- return_answer:
{"tool": "return_answer", "args": {"answer": "…final plain-text answer…"}}

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Among repositories that do not use Python, what proportion of their README.md files include copyright information?

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. metadata_database  
   - This database is stored in SQLite database format and contains metadata about GitHub repositories, including their declared programming languages, licenses, and repository-level statistics. It consists of three tables:  
   - This database consists of three tables:
    - languages  
      - Fields:  
        - repo_name (str): Name of the GitHub repository in `owner/repo` format.  
        - language_description (str): Programming language(s) used in the repository, represented in natural language format.  

    - licenses  
      - Fields:  
        - repo_name (str): Name of the GitHub repository in `owner/repo` format.  
        - license (str): License identifier associated with the repository (e.g., `apache-2.0`, `mit`).  

    - repos  
      - Fields:  
        - repo_name (str): Name of the GitHub repository in `owner/repo` format.  
        - watch_count (int): Number of users watching the repository on GitHub.  


2. artifacts_database  
   - This database is stored in DuckDB format and contains GitHub repository artifacts, including file contents, commit history, and file-level metadata. It consists of three tables:  
   - This database consists of three tables:
    - contents  
      - Fields:  
        - id (str): Identifier of the file blob in the repository.  
        - content (str): The file content (textual data). For large or binary files, this may contain placeholders or truncated values.  
        - sample_repo_name (str): Name of the repository where the file resides, in `owner/repo` format.  
        - sample_ref (str): Reference name (e.g., branch or commit SHA).  
        - sample_path (str): Path of the file within the repository.  
        - sample_symlink_target (str): If the file is a symlink, indicates the symlink target path.  
        - repo_data_description (str): Natural language description summarizing the file’s metadata (derived from original size, binary, copies, and mode fields).  

    - commits  
      - Fields:  
        - commit (str): Unique identifier (SHA) of the commit.  
        - tree (str): SHA identifier of the commit’s associated tree object.  
        - parent (str): One or more parent commit SHAs (JSON-like format for merge commits).  
        - author (str): JSON-like object containing author information (name, email, timestamp).  
        - committer (str): JSON-like object containing committer information (name, email, timestamp).  
        - subject (str): Short subject line of the commit message.  
        - message (str): Full commit message.  
        - trailer (str): Additional metadata fields attached to the commit (JSON-like).  
        - difference (str): JSON-like structure representing file changes introduced by the commit.  
        - difference_truncated (bool): Indicator if the difference data is truncated.  
        - repo_name (str): Repository name in `owner/repo` format.  
        - encoding (str): Encoding format of the commit data (if applicable).  

    - files  
      - Fields:  
        - repo_name (str): Name of the GitHub repository in `owner/repo` format.  
        - ref (str): Reference name (branch or commit SHA) at which the file metadata is recorded.  
        - path (str): Path of the file within the repository.  
        - mode (int): File mode (e.g., normal file, executable, symlink).  
        - id (str): Identifier of the file blob.  
        - symlink_target (str): If the file is a symlink, the target path it points to.

HINTS:
- Some queries may require joining across tables using identifiers such as "id" or "repo_name" to correctly combine information.  
- The "languages" table's language_description field may contain multiple programming languages per repository. To determine the primary or main language, compare the relative number of bytes across languages.  
- The "contents" table's repo_data_description field contains natural language metadata derived from file attributes (e.g., size, binary, copies, mode). Some queries may rely on these attributes for filtering or interpretation.

[{"id": "a230555c7a43e56ed55b96d9256ce7097142207f", "content": "![Travis report](https://travis-ci.org/ninja-ide/ninja-ide.svg?branch=master "Travis-C.I. Testing report")

# Ninja-ide Is Not Just Another IDE.
**Ninja-IDE** is a cross-platform integrated development environment (IDE) that allows developers to create applications for any purpose making the task of writing software easier and more enjoyable. It's also a secret ninja agency but this doesn't matter right now.
![Ninja-IDE logo](http://ninjaide.webfactional.com/static/common/img/ninja-big.png)


## Platforms
- Linux/X11
- Mac OS X
- Windows
- BSD


## Ninja contact
-   [Ninja website](http://ninja-ide.org "http://ninja-ide.org") at ninja-ide.org
-   [Mailing List](http://groups.google.com/group/ninja-ide/topics "Ninja Google Groups") at Google Groups
-   [@ninja\\_ide](https://twitter.com/ninja_ide "@ninja_ide") at Twitter
-   [+Ninja-IDE](https://plus.google.com/103973182574871451647 "Ninja-IDE at Google Plus") at Google Plus
-   [Ninja-IDE](https://kiwiirc.com/client/chat.freenode.net/?nick=Ninja%7C?&theme=cli#ninja-ide "ninja-ide at Freenode.net") at Freenode.net


## Requirements
On any system you want **Ninja-IDE**, you'll need to have this dependencies installed:

-   [Python](http://python.org "Python Homepage") >= 2.7  *(or Python3)*
-   [PyQt4](http://www.riverbankcomputing.com/software/pyqt/intro "PyQt Homepage") >= 4.8  *(Not Qt5)*
-   [PyQt4-QScintilla2](http://www.riverbankcomputing.com/software/qscintilla/intro "QScintilla2 Homepage") >= 2.0  *(Not Qt5)*
-   [PIP](https://pip.pypa.io/en/latest/installing.html "About Installing PIP")  *(Not PIP3)*
-   [Virtualenv](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/virtualenv "About Installing Virtualenv")


## Installing on Mac OS
```bash
brew install qt pyqt sip qscintilla2
```


## Cloning and Running
You can clone this repo and simply execute:

```bash
git clone git://github.com/ninja-ide/ninja-ide.git
cd ninja-ide
sudo pip install -r requirements.txt
python ninja-ide.py
```

Piece of cake, huh?


## Source Code API Documentation
- [http://ninja-ide.github.io/ninja-ide](http://ninja-ide.github.io/ninja-ide "Source Code API Documentation")


## Videos
[![Ninja-IDE Videos and Screencasts](http://img.youtube.com/vi/xShpNY5w-64/0.jpg)](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCPopm5397ozfsS8FOSSOWGQ "Ninja-IDE Videos and Screencasts")


## License
-   **GPLv3+** *(GPLv3 or any other version later published by FSF at your option)*
", "sample_repo_name": "ninja-ide/ninja-ide", "sample_ref": "refs/heads/master", "sample_path": "README.md", "sample_symlink_target": "None", "repo_data_description": "A 2401-byte non-binary file appearing 8 times, with sample mode code 33188."}, {"id": "1b7be69e7afd090f96a4ea04475a8254886bca66", "content": "# MIDI Synth

This application is a analog synthesizer simulation built on the [Web Audio API](https://dvcs.w3.org/hg/audio/raw-file/tip/webaudio/specification.html).  It is very loosely based on the architecture of a [Moog Prodigy](http://www.vintagesynth.com/moog/prodigy.php) synthesizer, although this is a polyphonic synthesizer, and it lacks the oscillator sync and glide effects of the Prodigy.  (AKA: this is not intended to be a replication of the Prodigy, so pleased don't tell me how crappy a reproduction it is! :)

This uses my [Web MIDI Polyfill](https://github.com/cwilso/WebMIDIAPIShim) to add MIDI support via the [Web MIDI API](http://webaudio.github.io/web-midi-api/) - in fact, I partly wrote this as a test case for the polyfill and the MIDI API itself, so if you have a MIDI keyboard attached, check it out.  The polyfill uses Java to access the MIDI device, so if you're wondering why Java is loading, that's why.  It may take a few seconds for MIDI to become active - the library takes a while to load - but when the ring turns gray (instead of blue), it's ready.  If you have a native implementation of the Web MIDI API in your browser, the polyfill shouldn't load - at the time of this writing, Chrome Stable (from version 43) has the only such implementation. Earlier versions of Chrome (from version 33) can enable Web MIDI via chrome://flags/#enable-web-midi

You can try it out live at https://webaudiodemos.appspot.com/midi-synth/index.html.

Check it out, feel free to fork, submit pull requests, etc.

-Chris

![logo](doc/doozer.png)

[![Build Status](https://secure.travis-ci.org/ha/doozerd.png)](http://travis-ci.org/ha/doozerd)

## What Is It?

Doozer is a highly-available, completely consistent
store for small amounts of extremely important data.
When the data changes, it can notify connected clients
immediately (no polling), making it ideal for
infrequently-updated data for which clients want
real-time updates. Doozer is good for name service,
database master elections, and configuration data shared
between several machines. See *When Should I Use It?*,
below, for details.

See the [mailing list][mail] to discuss doozer with
other users and developers.

## Quick Start

1. Download [doozerd](https://github.com/ha/doozerd/downloads)
2. Unpack the archive and put `doozerd` in your `PATH`
3. Repeat for [doozer](https://github.com/ha/doozer/downloads)
4. Start a doozerd with a WebView listening on `:8080`

        $ doozerd -w ":8080"

5. Set a key and read it back

        $ echo "hello, world" | doozer add /message
        $ doozer get /message
        hello, world

6. Open <http://localhost:8080> and see your message

![doozer web view](doc/webview.png)

## How Does It Work?

Doozer is a network service. A handful of machines
(usually three, five, or seven) each run one doozer
server process. These processes communicate with each
other using a standard fully-consistent distributed
consensus algorithm. Clients dial in to one or more of
the doozer servers, issue commands, such as GET, SET,
and WATCH, and receive responses.

(insert network diagram here)

Each doozerd process has a complete copy of the
datastore and serves both read and write requests; there
is no distinguished "master" or "leader". Doozer is
designed to store data that fits entirely in memory; it
never writes data to permanent files. A separate tool
provides durable storage for backup and recovery.

## When Should I Use It?

Here are some example scenarios:

1. *Name Service*

    You have a set of machines that serve incoming HTTP
    requests. Due to hardware failure, occasionally one
    of these machines will fail and you replace it with a
    new machine at a new network address. A change to DNS
    data would take time to reach all clients, because
    the TTL of the old DNS record would cause it to
    remain in client caches for some time.

    Instead of DNS, you could use Doozer. Clients can
    subscribe to the names they are interested in, and
    they will get notified when any of those names’
    addresses change.

2. *Database Master Election*

    You are deploying a MySQL system. You want it to have
    high availability, so you add slaves on separate
    physical machines. When the master fails, you might
    promote one slave to become the new master. At any
    given time, clients need to know which machine is the
    master, and the slaves must coordinate with each
    other during failover.

    You can use doozer to store the address of the
    current master and all information necessary to
    coordinate failover.

3. *Configuration*

    You have processes on several different machines, and
    you want them all to use the same config file, which
    you must occasionally update. It is important that
    they all use the same configuration.

    Store the config file in doozer, and have the
    processes read their configuration directly from
    doozer.

## What can I do with it?

We have a detailed description of the [data model](doc/data-model.md).

For ways to manipulate or read the data, see the [protocol spec](doc/proto.md).

Try out doozer's fault-tolerance with some [fire drills](doc/firedrill.md).

## Similar Projects

Doozer is similar to the following pieces of software:

 * Apache Zookeeper <http://zookeeper.apache.org/>
 * Google Chubby <http://research.google.com/archive/chubby.html>

## Hacking on Doozer

 * [hacking on doozer](doc/hacking.md)
 * [mailing list][mail]

Doozer is distributed under the terms of the MIT
License. See [LICENSE](LICENSE) for details.

Doozer was created by Blake Mizerany and Keith Rarick.

## Platforms

"}, {"sample_repo_name": "cwilso/midi-synth", "content": "# MIDI Synth

This application is a analog synthesizer simulation built on the [Web Audio API](https://dvcs.w3.org/hg/audio/raw-file/tip/webaudio/specification.html).  It is very loosely based on the architecture of a [Moog Prodigy](http://www.vintagesynth.com/moog/prodigy.php) synthesizer, although this is a polyphonic synthesizer, and it lacks the oscillator sync and glide effects of the Prodigy.  (AKA: this is not intended to be a replication of the Prodigy, so pleased don't tell me how crappy a reproduction it is! :)

This uses my [Web MIDI Polyfill](https://github.com/cwilso/WebMIDIAPIShim) to add MIDI support via the [Web MIDI API](http://webaudio.github.io/web-midi-api/) - in fact, I partly wrote this as a test case for the polyfill and the MIDI API itself, so if you have a MIDI keyboard attached, check it out.  The polyfill uses Java to access the MIDI device, so if you're wondering why Java is loading, that's why.  It may take a few seconds for MIDI to become active - the library takes a while to load - but when the ring turns gray (instead of blue), it's ready.  If you have a native implementation of the Web MIDI API in your browser, the polyfill shouldn't load - at the time of this writing, Chrome Stable (from version 43) has the only such implementation. Earlier versions of Chrome (from version 33) can enable Web MIDI via chrome://flags/#enable-web-midi

You can try it out live at https://webaudiodemos.appspot.com/midi-synth/index.html.

Check it out, feel free to fork, submit pull requests, etc.

-Chris
"}, {"sample_repo_name": "ha/doozerd", "content": "# Doozer

![logo](doc/doozer.png)

[![Build Status](https://secure.travis-ci.org/ha/doozerd.png)](http://travis-ci.org/ha/doozerd)

## What Is It?

Doozer is a highly-available, completely consistent
store for small amounts of extremely important data.
When the data changes, it can notify connected clients
immediately (no polling), making it ideal for
infrequently-updated data for which clients want
real-time updates. Doozer is good for name service,
database master elections, and configuration data shared
between several machines. See *When Should I Use It?*,
below, for details.

See the [mailing list][mail] to discuss doozer with
other users and developers.

## Quick Start

1. Download [doozerd](https://github.com/ha/doozerd/downloads)
2. Unpack the archive and put `doozerd` in your `PATH`
3. Repeat for [doozer](https://github.com/ha/doozer/downloads)
4. Start a doozerd with a WebView listening on `:8080`

        $ doozerd -w ":8080"

5. Set a key and read it back

        $ echo "hello, world" | doozer add /message
        $ doozer get /message
        hello, world

6. Open <http://localhost:8080> and see your message

![doozer web view](doc/webview.png)

## How Does It Work?

Doozer is a network service. A handful of machines
(usually three, five, or seven) each run one doozer
server process. These processes communicate with each
other using a standard fully-consistent distributed
consensus algorithm. Clients dial in to one or more of
the doozer servers, issue commands, such as GET, SET,
and WATCH, and receive responses.

(insert network diagram here)

Each doozerd process has a complete copy of the
datastore and serves both read and write requests; there
is no distinguished "master" or "leader". Doozer is
designed to store data that fits entirely in memory; it
never writes data to permanent files. A separate tool
provides durable storage for backup and recovery.

## When Should I Use It?

Here are some example scenarios:

1. *Name Service*

    You have a set of machines that serve incoming HTTP
    requests. Due to hardware failure, occasionally one
    of these machines will fail and you replace it with a
    new machine at a new network address. A change to DNS
    data would take time to reach all clients, because
    the TTL of the old DNS record would cause it to
    remain in client caches for some time.

    Instead of DNS, you could use Doozer. Clients can
    subscribe to the names they are interested in, and
    they will get notified when any of those names’
    addresses change.

2. *Database Master Election*

    You are deploying a MySQL system. You want it to have
    high availability, so you add slaves on separate
    physical machines. When the master fails, you might
    promote one slave to become the new master. At any
    given time, clients need to know which machine is the
    master, and the slaves must coordinate with each
    other during failover.

    You can use doozer to store the address of the
    current master and all information necessary to
    coordinate failover.

3. *Configuration*

    You have processes on several different machines, and
    you want them all to use the same config file, which
    you must occasionally update. It is important that
    they all use the same configuration.

    Store the config file in doozer, and have the
    processes read their configuration directly from
    doozer.

## What can I do with it?

We have a detailed description of the [data model](doc/data-model.md).

For ways to manipulate or read the data, see the [protocol spec](doc/proto.md).

Try out doozer's fault-tolerance with some [fire drills](doc/firedrill.md).

## Similar Projects

Doozer is similar to the following pieces of software:

 * Apache Zookeeper <http://zookeeper.apache.org/>
 * Google Chubby <http://research.google.com/archive/chubby.html>

## Hacking on Doozer

 * [hacking on doozer](doc/hacking.md)
 * [mailing list][mail]

## License and Authors

Doozer is distributed under the terms of the MIT
License. See [LICENSE](LICENSE) for details.

Doozer was created by Blake Mizerany and Keith Rarick.
Type `git shortlog -s` for a full list of contributors.

[mail]: https://groups.google.com/group/doozer

# ChatMessageView
ChatMessageView helps you to create chat message view quickly like a typical chatting application.
Its a container view, so you can add any type of message such as TextView or any customize TextView, ImageView, etc.


## Features
1. Can have any child inside of it.

3. Adjustable arrow position (top, bottom, left, right)

### Installation
add gradle dependency to your dependency list:

``` groovy
dependencies {
	compile 'me.himanshusoni.chatmessageview:chat-message-view:1.0.2'
}
```

### Use
1. Include `ChatMessageView` in your xml of adapter view with content inside.

``` xml
<me.himanshusoni.chatmessageview.ChatMessageView
	xmlns:app="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res-auto"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    app:cmv_backgroundColor="#88BABABA"
    app:cmv_backgroundColorPressed="

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re

# Get the data
non_python_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:4']
readme_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']

# Read the files
with open(non_python_file, 'r') as f:
    non_python_repos = [item['repo_name'] for item in json.load(f)]

with open(readme_file, 'r') as f:
    readme_data = json.load(f)

# Convert to set for faster lookup
non_python_set = set(non_python_repos)

# Filter READMEs that belong to non-Python repos
non_python_readmes = []
for item in readme_data:
    if item['sample_repo_name'] in non_python_set:
        non_python_readmes.append(item)

# Function to check for copyright
def has_copyright(content):
    if not content:
        return False
    return bool(re.search(r'copyright', content, re.IGNORECASE))

# Analyze
total_non_python_readmes = len(non_python_readmes)
copyright_count = sum(1 for item in non_python_readmes if has_copyright(item['content']))

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'total_non_python_readmes': total_non_python_readmes,
    'copyright_count': copyright_count,
    'sample_repos_with_copyright': [item['sample_repo_name'] for item in non_python_readmes if has_copyright(item['content'])][:5],
    'sample_repos_without_copyright': [item['sample_repo_name'] for item in non_python_readmes if not has_copyright(item['content'])][:5]
}))"}

message.reasoning:
I notice that the number of README.md files is very small (128). Let me check why this might be and explore more effectively. I think the issue might be that we need to process this in batches or use a different approach. Let me try to see how many total README.md files exist and whether I can work with this limited dataset.

Actually, looking at the problem differently, I have:
1. 2.7M repositories that don't use Python
2. Only 128 README.md files in the artifacts database

This is a problem - the artifacts database might be a sampled subset. Let me check which of these 128 README files belong to non-Python repos, and then determine what proportion contain copyright information.

Let me proceed with analyzing the data I have. I should:
1. Check which of the 128 README files belong to non-Python repos
2. Check which ones contain copyright information (case-insensitive search for "copyright")
3. Calculate the proportion

Let me also verify if there's a better way to match the repos.

messages.content:
You are a data analysis agent. Use only the tools listed below to answer the user's query, based on the provided DATABASE DESCRIPTION for logical database names and their types (SQL or MongoDB), and the results of previous tool calls.

TOOLS (system will execute):
- query_db: run a SQL or Mongo query. Returns a list of JSON-serializable records or an error string.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>", "query": "<SQL or Mongo query>"}
- list_db: list tables or collections for a given database.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>"}
- execute_python: run Python code.
  Required args: {"code": "<python_code>"}
- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).
  Required args: {"answer": "<final plain-text answer>"}

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. After each tool call, its result will be stored in a storage under a key named after the tool call id (you will be told the key name). The next message will include both the result (or a preview if it's large) and the storage key name.
2. Inside execute_python code you may read storage entries using the provided key names, e.g., if the tool call id is 'functions.tool:id', you can access its result via `locals()['var_functions.tool:id']` in your code.
3. You cannot modify or reassign those storage-provided variables; you may read them and create new variables as needed.
4. If a tool result is large, the next message will include a preview (first 10000 characters) and the storage entry will be the .json file path (a string) where the full result is stored. To access the full result, your execute_python code must open and read that .json file.

KEY RULES (must follow exactly):
1. Always use tool calls. Do not output plain text, explanations, or reasoning.
2. Include all required arguments for the tool you call.
3. For query_db, always specify db_name and query. Refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION for db_name and query format.
4. For PostgreSQL, wrap mixed-case or uppercase column names in double quotes.
5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.
6. Use execute_python for data processing as needed. 
7. When using execute_python, your code will be quoted by triple double-quotes and passed as a string to `exec(...)` for execution in a Python 3.12 environment with only pandas and pyarrow installed. So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.
8. When using execute_python, your code must print the result at the end exactly as shown in the PRINT FORMAT section below. The printed result must be a string that can be successfully parsed by json.loads() without errors.

PRINT FORMAT (must match exactly):
----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----
print("__RESULT__:")
print(your_json_serializable_string_here)
----END PRINT FORMAT----

For simple types (int, float, str, bool, None), you may use json.dumps() to produce a valid JSON string.

QUERY:
Among repositories that do not use Python, what proportion of their README.md files include copyright information?

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. metadata_database  
   - This database is stored in SQLite database format and contains metadata about GitHub repositories, including their declared programming languages, licenses, and repository-level statistics. It consists of three tables:  
   - This database consists of three tables:
    - languages  
      - Fields:  
        - repo_name (str): Name of the GitHub repository in `owner/repo` format.  
        - language_description (str): Programming language(s) used in the repository, represented in natural language format.  

    - licenses  
      - Fields:  
        - repo_name (str): Name of the GitHub repository in `owner/repo` format.  
        - license (str): License identifier associated with the repository (e.g., `apache-2.0`, `mit`).  

    - repos  
      - Fields:  
        - repo_name (str): Name of the GitHub repository in `owner/repo` format.  
        - watch_count (int): Number of users watching the repository on GitHub.  


2. artifacts_database  
   - This database is stored in DuckDB format and contains GitHub repository artifacts, including file contents, commit history, and file-level metadata. It consists of three tables:  
   - This database consists of three tables:
    - contents  
      - Fields:  
        - id (str): Identifier of the file blob in the repository.  
        - content (str): The file content (textual data). For large or binary files, this may contain placeholders or truncated values.  
        - sample_repo_name (str): Name of the repository where the file resides, in `owner/repo` format.  
        - sample_ref (str): Reference name (e.g., branch or commit SHA).  
        - sample_path (str): Path of the file within the repository.  
        - sample_symlink_target (str): If the file is a symlink, indicates the symlink target path.  
        - repo_data_description (str): Natural language description summarizing the file’s metadata (derived from original size, binary, copies, and mode fields).  

    - commits  
      - Fields:  
        - commit (str): Unique identifier (SHA) of the commit.  
        - tree (str): SHA identifier of the commit’s associated tree object.  
        - parent (str): One or more parent commit SHAs (JSON-like format for merge commits).  
        - author (str): JSON-like object containing author information (name, email, timestamp).  
        - committer (str): JSON-like object containing committer information (name, email, timestamp).  
        - subject (str): Short subject line of the commit message.  
        - message (str): Full commit message.  
        - trailer (str): Additional metadata fields attached to the commit (JSON-like).  
        - difference (str): JSON-like structure representing file changes introduced by the commit.  
        - difference_truncated (bool): Indicator if the difference data is truncated.  
        - repo_name (str): Repository name in `owner/repo` format.  
        - encoding (str): Encoding format of the commit data (if applicable).  

    - files  
      - Fields:  
        - repo_name (str): Name of the GitHub repository in `owner/repo` format.  
        - ref (str): Reference name (branch or commit SHA) at which the file metadata is recorded.  
        - path (str): Path of the file within the repository.  
        - mode (int): File mode (e.g., normal file, executable, symlink).  
        - id (str): Identifier of the file blob.  
        - symlink_target (str): If the file is a symlink, the target path it points to.

HINTS:
- Some queries may require joining across tables using identifiers such as "id" or "repo_name" to correctly combine information.  
- The "languages" table's language_description field may contain multiple programming languages per repository. To determine the primary or main language, compare the relative number of bytes across languages.  
- The "contents" table's repo_data_description field contains natural language metadata derived from file attributes (e.g., size, binary, copies, mode). Some queries may rely on these attributes for filtering or interpretation.

[{"id": "a230555c7a43e56ed55b96d9256ce7097142207f", "content": "![Travis report](https://travis-ci.org/ninja-ide/ninja-ide.svg?branch=master "Travis-C.I. Testing report")

# Ninja-ide Is Not Just Another IDE.
**Ninja-IDE** is a cross-platform integrated development environment (IDE) that allows developers to create applications for any purpose making the task of writing software easier and more enjoyable. It's also a secret ninja agency but this doesn't matter right now.
![Ninja-IDE logo](http://ninjaide.webfactional.com/static/common/img/ninja-big.png)


## Platforms
- Linux/X11
- Mac OS X
- Windows
- BSD


## Ninja contact
-   [Ninja website](http://ninja-ide.org "http://ninja-ide.org") at ninja-ide.org
-   [Mailing List](http://groups.google.com/group/ninja-ide/topics "Ninja Google Groups") at Google Groups
-   [@ninja\\_ide](https://twitter.com/ninja_ide "@ninja_ide") at Twitter
-   [+Ninja-IDE](https://plus.google.com/103973182574871451647 "Ninja-IDE at Google Plus") at Google Plus
-   [Ninja-IDE](https://kiwiirc.com/client/chat.freenode.net/?nick=Ninja%7C?&theme=cli#ninja-ide "ninja-ide at Freenode.net") at Freenode.net


## Requirements
On any system you want **Ninja-IDE**, you'll need to have this dependencies installed:

-   [Python](http://python.org "Python Homepage") >= 2.7  *(or Python3)*
-   [PyQt4](http://www.riverbankcomputing.com/software/pyqt/intro "PyQt Homepage") >= 4.8  *(Not Qt5)*
-   [PyQt4-QScintilla2](http://www.riverbankcomputing.com/software/qscintilla/intro "QScintilla2 Homepage") >= 2.0  *(Not Qt5)*
-   [PIP](https://pip.pypa.io/en/latest/installing.html "About Installing PIP")  *(Not PIP3)*
-   [Virtualenv](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/virtualenv "About Installing Virtualenv")


## Installing on Mac OS
```bash
brew install qt pyqt sip qscintilla2
```


## Cloning and Running
You can clone this repo and simply execute:

```bash
git clone git://github.com/ninja-ide/ninja-ide.git
cd ninja-ide
sudo pip install -r requirements.txt
python ninja-ide.py
```

Piece of cake, huh?


## Source Code API Documentation
- [http://ninja-ide.github.io/ninja-ide](http://ninja-ide.github.io/ninja-ide "Source Code API Documentation")


## Videos
[![Ninja-IDE Videos and Screencasts](http://img.youtube.com/vi/xShpNY5w-64/0.jpg)](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCPopm5397ozfsS8FOSSOWGQ "Ninja-IDE Videos and Screencasts")

", "sample_repo_name": "ninja-ide/ninja-ide", "sample_ref": "refs/heads/master", "sample_path": "README.md", "sample_symlink_target": "None", "repo_data_description": "A 2401-byte non-binary file appearing 8 times, with sample mode code 33188."}, {"id": "1b7be69e7afd090f96a4ea04475a8254886bca66", "content": "# MIDI Synth

This application is a analog synthesizer simulation built on the [Web Audio API](https://dvcs.w3.org/hg/audio/raw-file/tip/webaudio/specification.html).  It is very loosely based on the architecture of a [Moog Prodigy](http://www.vintagesynth.com/moog/prodigy.php) synthesizer, although this is a polyphonic synthesizer, and it lacks the oscillator sync and glide effects of the Prodigy.  (AKA: this is not intended to be a replication of the Prodigy, so pleased don't tell me how crappy a reproduction it is! :)

This uses my [Web MIDI Polyfill](https://github.com/cwilso/WebMIDIAPIShim) to add MIDI support via the [Web MIDI API](http://webaudio.github.io/web-midi-api/) - in fact, I partly wrote this as a test case for the polyfill and the MIDI API itself, so if you have a MIDI keyboard attached, check it out.  The polyfill uses Java to access the MIDI device, so if you're wondering why Java is loading, that's why.  It may take a few seconds for MIDI to become active - the library takes a while to load - but when the ring turns gray (instead of blue), it's ready.  If you have a native implementation of the Web MIDI API in your browser, the polyfill shouldn't load - at the time of this writing, Chrome Stable (from version 43) has the only such implementation. Earlier versions of Chrome (from version 33) can enable Web MIDI via chrome://flags/#enable-web-midi

You can try it out live at https://webaudiodemos.appspot.com/midi-synth/index.html.

Check it out, feel free to fork, submit pull requests, etc.

-Chris
", "sample_repo_name": "cwilso/midi-synth", "sample_ref": "refs/heads/master", "sample_path": "README.md", "sample_symlink_target": "None", "repo_data_description": "With a file size of 1542 bytes and sample mode 33188, this non-binary file is duplicated 9 times."}, {"id": "d7f62193ca0b588863bac5d2cb4b4caca5623194", "content": "# Doozer

![logo](doc/doozer.png)

[![Build Status](https://secure.travis-ci.org/ha/doozerd.png)](http://travis-ci.org/ha/doozerd)

## What Is It?

Doozer is a highly-available, completely consistent
store for small amounts of extremely important data.
When the data changes, it can notify connected clients
immediately (no polling), making it ideal for
infrequently-updated data for which clients want
real-time updates. Doozer is good for name service,
database master elections, and configuration data shared
between several machines. See *When Should I Use It?*,
below, for details.

See the [mailing list][mail] to discuss doozer with
other users and developers.

## Quick Start

1. Download [doozerd](https://github.com/ha/doozerd/downloads)
2. Unpack the archive and put `doozerd` in your `PATH`
3. Repeat for [doozer](https://github.com/ha/doozer/downloads)
4. Start a doozerd with a WebView listening on `:8080`

        $ doozerd -w ":8080"

5. Set a key and read it back

        $ echo "hello, world" | doozer add /message
        $ doozer get /message
        hello, world

6. Open <http://localhost:8080> and see your message

![doozer web view](doc/webview.png)

## How Does It Work?

Doozer is a network service. A handful of machines
(usually three, five, or seven) each run one doozer
server process. These processes communicate with each
other using a standard fully-consistent distributed
consensus algorithm. Clients dial in to one or more of
the doozer servers, issue commands, such as GET, SET,
and WATCH, and receive responses.

(insert network diagram here)

Each doozerd process has a complete copy of the
datastore and serves both read and write requests; there
is no distinguished "master" or "leader". Doozer is
designed to store data that fits entirely in memory; it
never writes data to permanent files. A separate tool
provides durable storage for backup and recovery.

## When Should I Use It?

Here are some example scenarios:

1. *Name Service*

    You have a set of machines that serve incoming HTTP
    requests. Due to hardware failure, occasionally one
    of these machines will fail and you replace it with a
    new machine at a new network address. A change to DNS
    data would take time to reach all clients, because
    the TTL of the old DNS record would cause it to
    remain in client caches for some time.

    Instead of DNS, you could use Doozer. Clients can
    subscribe to the names they are interested in, and
    they will get notified when any of those names’
    addresses change.

2. *Database Master Election*

    You are deploying a MySQL system. You want it to have
    high availability, so you add slaves on separate
    physical machines. When the master fails, you might
    promote one slave to become the new master. At any
    given time, clients need to know which machine is the
    master, and the slaves must coordinate with each
    other during failover.

    You can use doozer to store the address of the
    current master and all information necessary to
    coordinate failover.

3. *Configuration*

    You have processes on several different machines, and
    you want them all to use the same config file, which
    you must occasionally update. It is important that
    they all use the same configuration.

    Store the config file in doozer, and have the
    processes read their configuration directly from
    doozer.

## What can I do with it?

We have a detailed description of the [data model](doc/data-model.md).

For ways to manipulate or read the data, see the [protocol spec](doc/proto.md).

Try out doozer's fault-tolerance with some [fire drills](doc/firedrill.md).

## Similar Projects

Doozer is similar to the following pieces of software:

 * Apache Zookeeper <http://zookeeper.apache.org/>
 * Google Chubby <http://research.google.com/archive/chubby.html>

## Hacking on Doozer

 * [hacking on doozer](doc/hacking.md)
 * [mailing list][mail]

## License and Authors

Doozer is distributed under the terms of the MIT
License. See [LICENSE](LICENSE) for details.

Doozer was created by Blake Mizerany and Keith Rarick.
Type `git shortlog -s` for a full list of contributors.

# Ninja-ide Is Not Just Another IDE.
**Ninja-IDE** is a cross-platform integrated development environment (IDE) that allows developers to create applications for any purpose making the task of writing software easier and more enjoyable. It's also a secret ninja agency but this doesn't matter right now.
![Ninja-IDE logo](http://ninjaide.webfactional.com/static/common/img/ninja-big.png)


## Platforms
- Linux/X11
- Mac OS X
- Windows
- BSD


## Ninja contact
-   [Ninja website](http://ninja-ide.org "http://ninja-ide.org") at ninja-ide.org
-   [Mailing List](http://groups.google.com/group/ninja-ide/topics "Ninja Google Groups") at Google Groups
-   [@ninja\\_ide](https://twitter.com/ninja_ide "@ninja_ide") at Twitter
-   [+Ninja-IDE](https://plus.google.com/103973182574871451647 "Ninja-IDE at Google Plus") at Google Plus
-   [Ninja-IDE](https://kiwiirc.com/client/chat.freenode.net/?nick=Ninja%7C?&theme=cli#ninja-ide "ninja-ide at Freenode.net") at Freenode.net


## Requirements
On any system you want **Ninja-IDE**, you'll need to have this dependencies installed:

-   [Python](http://python.org "Python Homepage") >= 2.7  *(or Python3)*
-   [PyQt4](http://www.riverbankcomputing.com/software/pyqt/intro "PyQt Homepage") >= 4.8  *(Not Qt5)*
-   [PyQt4-QScintilla2](http://www.riverbankcomputing.com/software/qscintilla/intro "QScintilla2 Homepage") >= 2.0  *(Not Qt5)*
-   [PIP](https://pip.pypa.io/en/latest/installing.html "About Installing PIP")  *(Not PIP3)*
-   [Virtualenv](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/virtualenv "About Installing Virtualenv")


## Installing on Mac OS
```bash
brew install qt pyqt sip qscintilla2
```


## Cloning and Running
You can clone this repo and simply execute:

```bash
git clone git://github.com/ninja-ide/ninja-ide.git
cd ninja-ide
sudo pip install -r requirements.txt
python ninja-ide.py
```

Piece of cake, huh?


## Source Code API Documentation
- [http://ninja-ide.github.io/ninja-ide](http://ninja-ide.github.io/ninja-ide "Source Code API Documentation")


## Videos
[![Ninja-IDE Videos and Screencasts](http://img.youtube.com/vi/xShpNY5w-64/0.jpg)](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCPopm5397ozfsS8FOSSOWGQ "Ninja-IDE Videos and Screencasts")

-   **GPLv3+** *(GPLv3 or any other version later published by FSF at your option)*
"}, {"sample_repo_name": "cwilso/midi-synth", "content": "# MIDI Synth

This application is a analog synthesizer simulation built on the [Web Audio API](https://dvcs.w3.org/hg/audio/raw-file/tip/webaudio/specification.html).  It is very loosely based on the architecture of a [Moog Prodigy](http://www.vintagesynth.com/moog/prodigy.php) synthesizer, although this is a polyphonic synthesizer, and it lacks the oscillator sync and glide effects of the Prodigy.  (AKA: this is not intended to be a replication of the Prodigy, so pleased don't tell me how crappy a reproduction it is! :)

This uses my [Web MIDI Polyfill](https://github.com/cwilso/WebMIDIAPIShim) to add MIDI support via the [Web MIDI API](http://webaudio.github.io/web-midi-api/) - in fact, I partly wrote this as a test case for the polyfill and the MIDI API itself, so if you have a MIDI keyboard attached, check it out.  The polyfill uses Java to access the MIDI device, so if you're wondering why Java is loading, that's why.  It may take a few seconds for MIDI to become active - the library takes a while to load - but when the ring turns gray (instead of blue), it's ready.  If you have a native implementation of the Web MIDI API in your browser, the polyfill shouldn't load - at the time of this writing, Chrome Stable (from version 43) has the only such implementation. Earlier versions of Chrome (from version 33) can enable Web MIDI via chrome://flags/#enable-web-midi

You can try it out live at https://webaudiodemos.appspot.com/midi-synth/index.html.

Check it out, feel free to fork, submit pull requests, etc.

-Chris

"}, {"sample_repo_name": "devsoulwolf/ChatMessageView", "content": "[![Android Gems](http://www.android-gems.com/badge/himanshu-soni/ChatMessageView.svg?branch=master)](http://www.android-gems.com/lib/himanshu-soni/ChatMessageView)

# ChatMessageView
ChatMessageView helps you to create chat message view quickly like a typical chatting application.
Its a container view, so you can add any type of message such as TextView or any customize TextView, ImageView, etc.


## Features
1. Can have any child inside of it.
2. You can change color of `ChatMessageView` normal and pressed.
3. Adjustable arrow position (top, bottom, left, right)
4. Adjustable arrow gravity (start, end, center)
5. Chat view without arrow

### Sample Screen
![ChatMessageView](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/himanshu-soni/ChatMessageView/master/screenshot/screen2.jpg)

### Installation
add gradle dependency to your dependency list:

``` groovy
dependencies {
	compile 'me.himanshusoni.chatmessageview:chat-message-view:1.0.2'
}
```

### Use
1. Include `ChatMessageView` in your xml of adapter view with content inside.

``` xml
<me.himanshusoni.chatmessageview.ChatMessageView
	xmlns:app="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res-auto"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    app:cmv_backgroundColor="#88BABABA"
    app:cmv_backgroundColorPressed="

{"code": "import json

# Get the list of non-Python repos
non_python_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:4']
readme_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']

# Read the files
with open(non_python_file, 'r') as f:
    non_python_repos = [item['repo_name'] for item in json.load(f)]

with open(readme_file, 'r') as f:
    readme_data = json.load(f)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'non_python_repos_count': len(non_python_repos),

message.reasoning:
The user asks: "Among repositories that do not use Python, what proportion of their README.md files include copyright information?"

So I need to:
1. Identify repositories that do not use Python (from metadata_database.languages)
2. Find their README.md files (from artifacts_database.contents)
3. Check which README.md files include copyright information
4. Calculate the proportion

From the analysis:
- Total non-Python repositories: 2,774,729 (from languages table)
- But we don't have README.md for all of them, only a sample of 128 README.md files total
- Of those, 101 belong to non-Python repositories
- Of those 101, 15 include copyright information

The key issue: The sample of README.md files is very small (128) compared to the total non-Python repos (2.7M). So we need to be careful about interpretation.

The question is about "proportion" - this could mean:
- Proportion among all non-Python repos (but we don't have README.md data for all)
- Proportion among non-Python repos that have README.md files (but we don't know how many have README.md)
- Proportion among the sampled README.md files from non-Python repos

The most honest answer is: Among the sampled README.md files from non-Python repositories, 15 out of 101 (approximately 14.9%) include copyright information.

But better would be to note the limitation: We only have README.md data for 101 non-Python repositories in the sample, and 15 of them include copyright information.

Let me calculate the exact proportion: 15/101 = 0.1485... = 14.85%

The answer should clearly state the limitation that we only have a sample of README.md files, not all repositories.

Among repositories that do not use Python, what proportion of their README.md files include copyright information?

You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. metadata_database  
   - This database is stored in SQLite database format and contains metadata about GitHub repositories, including their declared programming languages, licenses, and repository-level statistics. It consists of three tables:  
   - This database consists of three tables:
    - languages  
      - Fields:  
        - repo_name (str): Name of the GitHub repository in `owner/repo` format.  
        - language_description (str): Programming language(s) used in the repository, represented in natural language format.  

    - licenses  
      - Fields:  
        - repo_name (str): Name of the GitHub repository in `owner/repo` format.  
        - license (str): License identifier associated with the repository (e.g., `apache-2.0`, `mit`).  

    - repos  
      - Fields:  
        - repo_name (str): Name of the GitHub repository in `owner/repo` format.  
        - watch_count (int): Number of users watching the repository on GitHub.  


2. artifacts_database  
   - This database is stored in DuckDB format and contains GitHub repository artifacts, including file contents, commit history, and file-level metadata. It consists of three tables:  
   - This database consists of three tables:
    - contents  
      - Fields:  
        - id (str): Identifier of the file blob in the repository.  
        - content (str): The file content (textual data). For large or binary files, this may contain placeholders or truncated values.  
        - sample_repo_name (str): Name of the repository where the file resides, in `owner/repo` format.  
        - sample_ref (str): Reference name (e.g., branch or commit SHA).  
        - sample_path (str): Path of the file within the repository.  
        - sample_symlink_target (str): If the file is a symlink, indicates the symlink target path.  
        - repo_data_description (str): Natural language description summarizing the file’s metadata (derived from original size, binary, copies, and mode fields).  

    - commits  
      - Fields:  
        - commit (str): Unique identifier (SHA) of the commit.  
        - tree (str): SHA identifier of the commit’s associated tree object.  
        - parent (str): One or more parent commit SHAs (JSON-like format for merge commits).  
        - author (str): JSON-like object containing author information (name, email, timestamp).  
        - committer (str): JSON-like object containing committer information (name, email, timestamp).  
        - subject (str): Short subject line of the commit message.  
        - message (str): Full commit message.  
        - trailer (str): Additional metadata fields attached to the commit (JSON-like).  
        - difference (str): JSON-like structure representing file changes introduced by the commit.  
        - difference_truncated (bool): Indicator if the difference data is truncated.  
        - repo_name (str): Repository name in `owner/repo` format.  
        - encoding (str): Encoding format of the commit data (if applicable).  

    - files  
      - Fields:  
        - repo_name (str): Name of the GitHub repository in `owner/repo` format.  
        - ref (str): Reference name (branch or commit SHA) at which the file metadata is recorded.  
        - path (str): Path of the file within the repository.  
        - mode (int): File mode (e.g., normal file, executable, symlink).  
        - id (str): Identifier of the file blob.  
        - symlink_target (str): If the file is a symlink, the target path it points to.

HINTS:

- The "languages" table's language_description field may contain multiple programming languages per repository. To determine the primary or main language, compare the relative number of bytes across languages.  
- The "contents" table's repo_data_description field contains natural language metadata derived from file attributes (e.g., size, binary, copies, mode). Some queries may rely on these attributes for filtering or interpretation.

[{"id": "a230555c7a43e56ed55b96d9256ce7097142207f", "content": "![Travis report](https://travis-ci.org/ninja-ide/ninja-ide.svg?branch=master "Travis-C.I. Testing report")

# Ninja-ide Is Not Just Another IDE.
**Ninja-IDE** is a cross-platform integrated development environment (IDE) that allows developers to create applications for any purpose making the task of writing software easier and more enjoyable. It's also a secret ninja agency but this doesn't matter right now.
![Ninja-IDE logo](http://ninjaide.webfactional.com/static/common/img/ninja-big.png)


## Platforms
- Linux/X11
- Mac OS X
- Windows
- BSD


## Ninja contact
-   [Ninja website](http://ninja-ide.org "http://ninja-ide.org") at ninja-ide.org
-   [Mailing List](http://groups.google.com/group/ninja-ide/topics "Ninja Google Groups") at Google Groups
-   [@ninja\\_ide](https://twitter.com/ninja_ide "@ninja_ide") at Twitter
-   [+Ninja-IDE](https://plus.google.com/103973182574871451647 "Ninja-IDE at Google Plus") at Google Plus
-   [Ninja-IDE](https://kiwiirc.com/client/chat.freenode.net/?nick=Ninja%7C?&theme=cli#ninja-ide "ninja-ide at Freenode.net") at Freenode.net


## Requirements
On any system you want **Ninja-IDE**, you'll need to have this dependencies installed:

-   [Python](http://python.org "Python Homepage") >= 2.7  *(or Python3)*
-   [PyQt4](http://www.riverbankcomputing.com/software/pyqt/intro "PyQt Homepage") >= 4.8  *(Not Qt5)*
-   [PyQt4-QScintilla2](http://www.riverbankcomputing.com/software/qscintilla/intro "QScintilla2 Homepage") >= 2.0  *(Not Qt5)*
-   [PIP](https://pip.pypa.io/en/latest/installing.html "About Installing PIP")  *(Not PIP3)*
-   [Virtualenv](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/virtualenv "About Installing Virtualenv")


## Installing on Mac OS
```bash
brew install qt pyqt sip qscintilla2
```


## Cloning and Running
You can clone this repo and simply execute:

```bash
git clone git://github.com/ninja-ide/ninja-ide.git
cd ninja-ide
sudo pip install -r requirements.txt
python ninja-ide.py
```

Piece of cake, huh?


## Source Code API Documentation
- [http://ninja-ide.github.io/ninja-ide](http://ninja-ide.github.io/ninja-ide "Source Code API Documentation")


## Videos
[![Ninja-IDE Videos and Screencasts](http://img.youtube.com/vi/xShpNY5w-64/0.jpg)](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCPopm5397ozfsS8FOSSOWGQ "Ninja-IDE Videos and Screencasts")


## License
-   **GPLv3+** *(GPLv3 or any other version later published by FSF at your option)*

This application is a analog synthesizer simulation built on the [Web Audio API](https://dvcs.w3.org/hg/audio/raw-file/tip/webaudio/specification.html).  It is very loosely based on the architecture of a [Moog Prodigy](http://www.vintagesynth.com/moog/prodigy.php) synthesizer, although this is a polyphonic synthesizer, and it lacks the oscillator sync and glide effects of the Prodigy.  (AKA: this is not intended to be a replication of the Prodigy, so pleased don't tell me how crappy a reproduction it is! :)

This uses my [Web MIDI Polyfill](https://github.com/cwilso/WebMIDIAPIShim) to add MIDI support via the [Web MIDI API](http://webaudio.github.io/web-midi-api/) - in fact, I partly wrote this as a test case for the polyfill and the MIDI API itself, so if you have a MIDI keyboard attached, check it out.  The polyfill uses Java to access the MIDI device, so if you're wondering why Java is loading, that's why.  It may take a few seconds for MIDI to become active - the library takes a while to load - but when the ring turns gray (instead of blue), it's ready.  If you have a native implementation of the Web MIDI API in your browser, the polyfill shouldn't load - at the time of this writing, Chrome Stable (from version 43) has the only such implementation. Earlier versions of Chrome (from version 33) can enable Web MIDI via chrome://flags/#enable-web-midi

You can try it out live at https://webaudiodemos.appspot.com/midi-synth/index.html.

Check it out, feel free to fork, submit pull requests, etc.

-Chris

![logo](doc/doozer.png)

[![Build Status](https://secure.travis-ci.org/ha/doozerd.png)](http://travis-ci.org/ha/doozerd)

## What Is It?

Doozer is a highly-available, completely consistent
store for small amounts of extremely important data.
When the data changes, it can notify connected clients
immediately (no polling), making it ideal for
infrequently-updated data for which clients want
real-time updates. Doozer is good for name service,
database master elections, and configuration data shared
between several machines. See *When Should I Use It?*,
below, for details.

See the [mailing list][mail] to discuss doozer with
other users and developers.

## Quick Start

1. Download [doozerd](https://github.com/ha/doozerd/downloads)
2. Unpack the archive and put `doozerd` in your `PATH`
3. Repeat for [doozer](https://github.com/ha/doozer/downloads)
4. Start a doozerd with a WebView listening on `:8080`

        $ doozerd -w ":8080"

5. Set a key and read it back

        $ echo "hello, world" | doozer add /message
        $ doozer get /message
        hello, world

6. Open <http://localhost:8080> and see your message

![doozer web view](doc/webview.png)

## How Does It Work?

Doozer is a network service. A handful of machines
(usually three, five, or seven) each run one doozer
server process. These processes communicate with each
other using a standard fully-consistent distributed
consensus algorithm. Clients dial in to one or more of
the doozer servers, issue commands, such as GET, SET,
and WATCH, and receive responses.

(insert network diagram here)

Each doozerd process has a complete copy of the
datastore and serves both read and write requests; there
is no distinguished "master" or "leader". Doozer is
designed to store data that fits entirely in memory; it
never writes data to permanent files. A separate tool
provides durable storage for backup and recovery.

## When Should I Use It?

Here are some example scenarios:

1. *Name Service*

    You have a set of machines that serve incoming HTTP
    requests. Due to hardware failure, occasionally one
    of these machines will fail and you replace it with a
    new machine at a new network address. A change to DNS
    data would take time to reach all clients, because
    the TTL of the old DNS record would cause it to
    remain in client caches for some time.

    Instead of DNS, you could use Doozer. Clients can
    subscribe to the names they are interested in, and
    they will get notified when any of those names’
    addresses change.

2. *Database Master Election*

    You are deploying a MySQL system. You want it to have
    high availability, so you add slaves on separate
    physical machines. When the master fails, you might
    promote one slave to become the new master. At any
    given time, clients need to know which machine is the
    master, and the slaves must coordinate with each
    other during failover.

    You can use doozer to store the address of the
    current master and all information necessary to
    coordinate failover.

3. *Configuration*

    You have processes on several different machines, and
    you want them all to use the same config file, which
    you must occasionally update. It is important that
    they all use the same configuration.

    Store the config file in doozer, and have the
    processes read their configuration directly from
    doozer.

## What can I do with it?

We have a detailed description of the [data model](doc/data-model.md).

For ways to manipulate or read the data, see the [protocol spec](doc/proto.md).

Try out doozer's fault-tolerance with some [fire drills](doc/firedrill.md).

## Similar Projects

Doozer is similar to the following pieces of software:

 * Apache Zookeeper <http://zookeeper.apache.org/>
 * Google Chubby <http://research.google.com/archive/chubby.html>

## Hacking on Doozer

 * [hacking on doozer](doc/hacking.md)
 * [mailing list][mail]

## License and Authors

Doozer is distributed under the terms of the MIT
License. See [LICENSE](LICENSE) for details.

Doozer was created by Blake Mizerany and Keith Rarick.
Type `git shortlog -s` for a full list of contributors.

[mail]: https://groups.google.com/group/doozer

# ChatMessageView
ChatMessageView helps you to create chat message view quickly like a typical chatting application.
Its a container view, so you can add any type of message such as TextView or any customize TextView, ImageView, etc.


## Features
1. Can have any child inside of it.
2. You can change color of `ChatMessageView` normal and pressed.
3. Adjustable arrow position

"}, {"sample_repo_name": "cwilso/midi-synth", "content": "# MIDI Synth

This application is a analog synthesizer simulation built on the [Web Audio API](https://dvcs.w3.org/hg/audio/raw-file/tip/webaudio/specification.html).  It is very loosely based on the architecture of a [Moog Prodigy](http://www.vintagesynth.com/moog/prodigy.php) synthesizer, although this is a polyphonic synthesizer, and it lacks the oscillator sync and glide effects of the Prodigy.  (AKA: this is not intended to be a replication of the Prodigy, so pleased don't tell me how crappy a reproduction it is! :)

This uses my [Web MIDI Polyfill](https://github.com/cwilso/WebMIDIAPIShim) to add MIDI support via the [Web MIDI API](http://webaudio.github.io/web-midi-api/) - in fact, I partly wrote this as a test case for the polyfill and the MIDI API itself, so if you have a MIDI keyboard attached, check it out.  The polyfill uses Java to access the MIDI device, so if you're wondering why Java is loading, that's why.  It may take a few seconds for MIDI to become active - the library takes a while to load - but when the ring turns gray (instead of blue), it's ready.  If you have a native implementation of the Web MIDI API in your browser, the polyfill shouldn't load - at the time of this writing, Chrome Stable (from version 43) has the only such implementation. Earlier versions of Chrome (from version 33) can enable Web MIDI via chrome://flags/#enable-web-midi

You can try it out live at https://webaudiodemos.appspot.com/midi-synth/index.html.

Check it out, feel free to fork, submit pull requests, etc.

-Chris

"}, {"sample_repo_name": "devsoulwolf/ChatMessageView", "content": "[![Android Gems](http://www.android-gems.com/badge/himanshu-soni/ChatMessageView.svg?branch=master)](http://www.android-gems.com/lib/himanshu-soni/ChatMessageView)

# ChatMessageView
ChatMessageView helps you to create chat message view quickly like a typical chatting application.
Its a container view, so you can add any type of message such as TextView or any customize TextView, ImageView, etc.


## Features
1. Can have any child inside of it.
2. You can change color of `ChatMessageView` normal and pressed.
3. Adjustable arrow position (top, bottom, left, right)
4. Adjustable arrow gravity (start, end, center)
5. Chat view without arrow

### Sample Screen
![ChatMessageView](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/himanshu-soni/ChatMessageView/master/screenshot/screen2.jpg)

### Installation
add gradle dependency to your dependency list:

``` groovy
dependencies {
	compile 'me.himanshusoni.chatmessageview:chat-message-view:1.0.2'
}
```

### Use
1. Include `ChatMessageView` in your xml of adapter view with content inside.

``` xml
<me.himanshusoni.chatmessageview.ChatMessageView
	xmlns:app="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res-auto"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    app:cmv_backgroundColor="#88BABABA"
    app:cmv_backgroundColorPressed="

It may take a few seconds for MIDI to become active - the library takes a while to load - but when the ring turns gray (instead of blue), it's ready.  If you have a native implementation of the Web MIDI API in your browser, the polyfill shouldn't load - at the time of this writing, Chrome Stable (from version 43) has the only such implementation. ## How Does It Work?

Doozer is a network service. A handful of machines
(usually three, five, or seven) each run one doozer
server process. These processes communicate with each
other using a standard fully-consistent distributed
consensus algorithm. Clients dial in to one or more of
the doozer servers, issue commands, such as GET, SET,
and WATCH, and receive responses.

(insert network diagram here)

Each doozerd process has a complete copy of the
datastore and serves both read and write requests; there
is no distinguished "master" or "leader". Doozer is
designed to store data that fits entirely in memory; it
never writes data to permanent files. A separate tool
provides durable storage for backup and recovery.

## When Should I Use It?

Here are some example scenarios:

1. *Name Service*

    You have a set of machines that serve incoming HTTP
    requests. Due to hardware failure, occasionally one
    of these machines will fail and you replace it with a
    new machine at a new network address. A change to DNS
    data would take time to reach all clients, because
    the TTL of the old DNS record would cause it to
    remain in client caches for some time.

    Instead of DNS, you could use Doozer. Clients can
    subscribe to the names they are interested in, and
    they will get notified when any of those names’
    addresses change.

2. *Database Master Election*

    You are deploying a MySQL system. You want it to have
    high availability, so you add slaves on separate
    physical machines. When the master fails, you might
    promote one slave to become the new master. At any
    given time, clients need to know which machine is the
    master, and the slaves must coordinate with each
    other during failover.

    You can use doozer to store the address of the
    current master and all information necessary to
    coordinate failover.

3. *Configuration*

    You have processes on several different machines, and
    you want them all to use the same config file, which
    you must occasionally update. It is important that
    they all use the same configuration.

    Store the config file in doozer, and have the
    processes read their configuration directly from
    doozer.

## What can I do with it?

We have a detailed description of the [data model](doc/data-model.md).

For ways to manipulate or read the data, see the [protocol spec](doc/proto.md).

Try out doozer's fault-tolerance with some [fire drills](doc/firedrill.md).

## Similar Projects

Doozer is similar to the following pieces of software:

 * Apache Zookeeper <http://zookeeper.apache.org/>
 * Google Chubby <http://research.google.com/archive/chubby.html>

## Hacking on Doozer

 * [hacking on doozer](doc/hacking.md)
 * [mailing list][mail]

# ChatMessageView
ChatMessageView helps you to create chat message view quickly like a typical chatting application.
Its a container view, so you can add any type of message such as TextView or any customize TextView, ImageView, etc.


## Features
1. Can have any child inside of it.
2. You can change color of `ChatMessageView` normal and pressed.
3. Adjustable arrow position (top, bottom, left, right)
4. Adjustable arrow gravity (start, end, center)
5. Chat view without arrow

### Sample Screen
![ChatMessageView](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/himanshu-soni/ChatMessageView/master/screenshot/screen2.jpg)

### Installation
add gradle dependency to your dependency list:

``` groovy
dependencies {
	compile 'me.himanshusoni.chatmessageview:chat-message-view:1.0.2'
}
```

### Use
1. Include `ChatMessageView` in your xml of adapter view with content inside.

``` xml
<me.himanshusoni.chatmessageview.ChatMessageView
	xmlns:app="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res-auto"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    app:cmv_backgroundColor="#88BABABA"

{"code": "import json
import re

# Get the data
non_python_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:4']
readme_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']

# Read the files
with open(non_python_file, 'r') as f:
    non_python_repos = [item['repo_name'] for item in json.load(f)]

with open(readme_file, 'r') as f:
    readme_data = json.load(f)

# Convert to set for faster lookup
non_python_set = set(non_python_repos)

# Filter READMEs that belong to non-Python repos
non_python_readmes = []
for item in readme_data:
    if item['sample_repo_name'] in non_python_set:
        non_python_readmes.append(item)

# Function to check for copyright
def has_copyright(content):
    if not content:
        return False
    return bool(re.search(r'copyright', content, re.IGNORECASE))

# Analyze
total_non_python_readmes = len(non_python_readmes)
copyright_count = sum(1 for item in non_python_readmes if has_copyright(item['content']))

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'total_non_python_readmes': total_non_python_readmes,
    'copyright_count': copyright_count,
    'sample_repos_with_copyright': [item['sample_repo_name'] for item in non_python_readmes if has_copyright(item['content'])][:5],
    'sample_repos_without_copyright': [item['sample_repo_name'] for item in non_python_readmes if not has_copyright(item['content'])][:5]
}))"}
